title: Nutrigenomics Effects of Fruits and Vegetables. On a fluctuating basis, dietary variations may lead to nutrigenomic responses. For example, dietary sugars, fats, and amino acids may control gene expression through carbohydrate-responsive element binding protein, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors, and G C N 2 and activating transcription factor 4 and m T O R C 1 pathways, respectively. Homeostasis may be maintained by such systems, but sustained alterations may lead to conditions such as obesity, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, or cancer.

sentences.text: Nutrigenomics Effects of Fruits and Vegetables.
On a fluctuating basis, dietary variations may lead to nutrigenomic responses.

For example, dietary sugars, fats, and amino acids may control gene expression through carbohydrate-responsive element binding protein, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors, and G C N 2 and activating transcription factor 4 and m T O R C 1 pathways, respectively.
Homeostasis may be maintained by such systems, but sustained alterations may lead to conditions such as obesity, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, or cancer.
As described below, diet as a whole has a significant influence on the genotypic expression of human beings.
In essence, changing the diet resets the homeostatic positioning of the individual.
In the case of the Mediterranean or Okinawan-based Nordic diet, this is generally perceived as for the better.

In addition to the impact of diet, a very long list of dietary food components, for example, vitamins such as vitamin D, vitamin A, vitamin E; polyphenols such as resveratrol, quercetin, catechins, curcumin; fatty acids such as omega-3 and omega-6; and minerals such as selenium and zinc, have been examined for potential to mediate various nutritional genomic responses, for example, gene expression, D N A methylation, histone modification, non-coding R N As, activation of transcription factors, ligand binding to the nuclear receptors.
We have adopted an intermediate approach between evaluating the effect of diet and evaluating the effect of individual dietary chemicals.
It is fully appreciated that homeostasis may be affected by numerous factors such as exercise, sleep, stress, toxins, and pathogens in addition to diet.
But nonetheless, diet is a dominant force, and the overall effect culminates from the overall diet.
In our work with grapes, the focus has been on the whole food, not individual components such as resveratrol.

While it is necessary to use a standardized product to assure scientific rigor, the standardized product is fully representative of grapes as a whole food, and we have taken care to administer quantities that are fully representative of quantities that are readily consumed by humans.

In sum, our work is aimed at exploring how the addition of grapes to the normal diet of a free-living human being may lead to nutrigenomic changes that may be interpreted in relation to a person's health or risk of developing diseases.
